chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number:
CSCE 689
Course Title:
Advanced Topics in Deep Reinforcement Learning
Section:
600
Time:
MWF, 8:00am - 8:50am
Location:
HRBB 124
Credit Hours:
3

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Deep Reinforcement Learning (Deep RL) lies at the intersection of reinforcement learning and
deep learning, and has emerged as one of the most impactful areas of artificial intelligence. By
enabling agents to learn complex behaviors directly from high-dimensional sensory data, Deep
RL has powered breakthroughs in areas such as game playing (e.g., AlphaGo, OpenAI Five),
robotics, natural language processing, scientific discovery, and autonomous systems. Beyond
these headline successes, Deep RL continues to drive fundamental advances in sequential
decision-making, representation learning, and human-AI collaboration, making it a cornerstone
of contemporary AI research.

This course, Advanced Topics in Deep Reinforcement Learning, is intended for graduate
students and advanced undergraduates who already have foundational knowledge in
reinforcement learning and machine learning. It is designed for students who wish to deepen
their understanding of modern Deep RL methods, critically engage with the research literature,
and develop the skills to evaluate and present scientific work.

The course will cover a wide range of Deep RL topics, including: Value-based and policy
gradient methods, Actor-critic architectures and stability challenges, Exploration and sample
efficiency, Continuous control, Model-based Deep RL, Hierarchical reinforcement learning,
Multi-agent Deep RL, Offline/batch RL and safe RL, Human-in-the-loop learning and
reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), Applications and frontiers of Deep RL in
real-world systems

chunks.title: Course Structure and Expectations
chunks.content:
The class will be conducted in a seminar format. The instructor will provide introductory
lectures in the first weeks to establish the necessary background, covering core algorithms,
theoretical foundations, and open challenges in Deep RL. Beginning in Week 4, students will
lead the discussion through research paper presentations. Each student will select a paper from
an approved list of seminal and contemporary works, prepare a 40-minute presentation
explaining the motivation, approach, contributions, and empirical findings, and then guide a 10-
minute Q&A session with their peers.

Students are expected to:

•
Engage actively with the assigned readings before class.
•
Provide thoughtful feedback and ask critical questions during peer presentations.
•
Deliver one high-quality presentation during the semester.
•
Complete a comprehensive final exam assessing their understanding of the covered
material.

Through this structure, the course emphasizes not only content mastery but also critical
analysis, scientific communication, and peer-to-peer learning, equipping students with the
skills needed for research and professional work in advanced Al domains.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

• Critically analyze state-of-the-art research papers in deep reinforcement learning, identifying their
motivations, methodologies, theoretical contributions, and empirical findings.
• Communicate research effectively by delivering a structured 40-minute presentation and responding
accurately to peer questions during discussion.
• Compare and contrast different approaches in deep reinforcement learning with respect to
algorithmic design, applicability, strengths, and limitations.
• Synthesize insights from multiple research papers to explain current trends, open challenges, and
emerging directions in deep reinforcement learning.
• Demonstrate mastery of course material by answering conceptual and applied questions on a
comprehensive final exam.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grades will be assigned according to the following scale:

•
A: 90-100%
•
B: 80-89%
•
C: 70-79%
•
D: 60-69%
•
F: < 60%

chunks.title: Graded Class Participation
chunks.content:
Since participation exceeds 10% of the course grade, the following rubric will apply:

•
Excellent (90–100%): Regularly contributes well-prepared, insightful questions and
comments that advance discussion; engages respectfully and critically with peers' work.
•
Good (80–89%): Frequently participates with relevant comments/questions;
demonstrates preparation and engagement.
•
Satisfactory (70–79%): Occasionally contributes; comments may lack depth or show
limited preparation.
•
Poor (60-69%): Rarely participates or contributions show minimal preparation.
•
Unsatisfactory (<60%): Absent from discussions or does not contribute.

chunks.title: Graded Attendance
chunks.content:
Attendance will be recorded at each seminar session. Because the seminar format relies on active
discussion, more than two unexcused absences will result in a 1% grade reduction per
unexcused absence. Excused absences must follow Student Rule 7 and require appropriate
documentation.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
•
Definition of Late Work: Any assignment, deliverable, or presentation component
submitted after the established deadline without an approved excused absence (as
defined in Student Rule 7) will be considered late.
•
Acceptance of Late Work:

•
Paper Presentation & Q&A: Because presentations are scheduled in advance
and central to the seminar format, late presentations will not be accepted.
Students with an excused absence will be rescheduled.
•
Final Exam: Makeup exams will only be provided for students with an excused
absence (Student Rule 7).
•
Other Written Deliverables (if applicable): Late submissions will be accepted
up to 48 hours after the deadline with a 10% deduction per 24 hours late.
Submissions more than 48 hours late will receive a grade of zero.
•
Excused Absences: Work missed due to an approved excused absence is exempt from
this late work policy and will be handled in accordance with Student Rule 7.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
| Week | Topic / Activity | Major Assignment / Exam |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Course introduction; Overview of Reinforcement Learning and Deep RL; Seminar expectations | --- |
| 2 | Background Lecture: Policy Gradient Methods and Actor-Critic Architectures | --- |
| 3 | Background Lecture: Exploration, Sample Efficiency, and Stability Challenges in Deep RL | Paper selection finalized |
| 4 | Value-Based Deep RL (e.g., DQN, Rainbow, Double Q-learning) | Student Presentations 1-2 |
| 5 | Policy Gradient and Actor-Critic Methods (e.g., TRPO, PPO, A3C) | Student Presentations 3-4 |
| 6 | Continuous Control (e.g., DDPG, SAC, TD3) | Student Presentations 5-6 |
| 7 | Exploration and Sample Efficiency (e.g., intrinsic motivation, prioritized replay) | Student Presentations 7-8 |
| 8 | Model-Based Deep RL (e.g., World Models, Dreamer, MuZero) | Student Presentations 9-10 |
| 9 | Hierarchical RL (e.g., options framework, FeUdal Networks, HIRO) | Student Presentations 11-12 |
| 10 | Multi-Agent Deep RL (e.g., COMA, QMIX, emergent communication) | Student Presentations 13-14 |
| 11 | Offline / Batch Deep RL (e.g., CQL, BEAR, BRAC) | Student Presentations 15-16 |
| 12 | Human-in-the-Loop and RLHF (e.g., Christiano et al. 2017, Ziegler et al. 2019, InstructGPT) | Student Presentations 17-18 |
| 13 | Deep RL in Games and Large-Scale Systems (e.g., AlphaGo, AlphaZero, OpenAI Five) | Student Presentations 19-20 |
| 14 | Applications and Frontiers (robotics, autonomous driving, scientific discovery, future challenges) | Student Presentations 21-22 |
| 15 | Final Exam (Comprehensive) | Final Exam |
